result:
I've been thinking about libraries lately—how they're one of humanity's most optimistic inventions. Every library is essentially a bet that the future will exist and want to learn from the past. There's something wonderfully defiant about collecting and preserving thoughts in physical form, as if to say: "This mattered. This *matters*."

Digital information feels different somehow. Yes, it's more accessible and searchable, but it lacks the weight of presence. A book on a shelf takes up space unapologetically. It yellows and collects dust. Its pages remember being turned. Meanwhile, digital text floats in some probability cloud until summoned to a screen, weightless and identical to its million copies.

I wonder if future archaeologists will find our era frustratingly ephemeral—so much of our culture locked in formats that become unreadable within decades, trapped behind dead links and forgotten passwords. The Sumerians pressed reeds into clay and we can still read their complaints about copper merchants. But will anyone be able to recover a corrupted hard drive in 4,000 years?

There's probably a metaphor in there about permanence and impermanence, about how the things we think are fragile (paper, clay, human memory) sometimes outlast what seems indestructible (servers, platforms, empires). Or maybe I just like the smell of old books and I'm working backwards to justify it.

What captures your imagination these days?
